metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the code-review gate produces no `overflow` bucket (verified: only {blocking, surfaced, dropped, indeterminate} from `code_review_decide`) and either remove 'overflow' from the What/AC/Scope description or explicitly spec the work needed to add overflow semantics to code-review.
evidence: code_review_decide (workflow_ops.py lines 428-450) partitions into buckets: {blocking, surfaced, dropped, indeterminate} — no overflow key anywhere
code_review_verdict.schema.json properties: {verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage, runner, model} — no overflow
plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload (line 353-358) does have overflow: `verdict.get('overflow', [])`, but code-review has no such step
Search for 'overflow' in code-review gate files found no matches
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'overflow' as a bucket that code-review produces ('dropped / indeterminate / overflow' in the What section and AC), but the code-review gate produces no 'overflow' bucket — `code_review_decide` only partitions into {blocking, surfaced, dropped, indeterminate}, and the schema (code_review_verdict.schema.json) has no overflow property.
findings.location: What section / Scope section ('pool ALL buckets (surfaced + dropped + indeterminate + overflow)') / AC line 1 ('dropped/indeterminate findings')
scenarios: When the plan author adds `verdict.get('overflow', [])` to the new v2 build_payload, it will always be an empty list because the code-review gate never populates it — the bucket silently disappears without error, making the feature description misleading.
A future caller reading a v2 sidecar expecting an overflow bucket to be populated will find it absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'overflow' from the bucket list in the What section and AC. Code-review has no cap-based overflow; the four partitions (blocking, advisory, dropped, indeterminate) cover all findings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope: update `code-review.yaml`'s `verdict` step (or a preceding consolidation step) to wire `steps.decide.outputs.dropped` and `steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate` through to the terminal verdict dict, so `build_payload` can actually read them; without this wiring the v2 sidecar will only see novelty-floor drops.
evidence: code-review.yaml step 'verdict' (uses: code_review_coach) with: inputs only `blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }}`, `surfaced: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}`, `notes: ${{ steps.coach.outputs.notes }}` — decide.outputs.dropped and decide.outputs.indeterminate are NOT wired
code_review_coach op (workflow_ops.py lines 491-509) returns {verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage} — no dropped/indeterminate
apply_region_gated_floor (workflow_ops.py line 634) writes verdict['dropped'] for novelty-floor drops only, NOT for Pass-3 drops
Approach rationale: 'the code-review gate already runs the identical four-pass kernel, so its findings already carry the pass-2/pass-3 fields; they are simply not all being written'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `dropped` and `indeterminate` findings 'already carry the pass-2/pass-3 fields; they are simply not all being written', implying they are present on the verdict dict at `build_payload` time. In reality `code_review_coach` (the terminal workflow output step) only assembles `{verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage}` from `decide.outputs.blocking` and `decide.outputs.surfaced` — `decide.outputs.dropped` and `decide.outputs.indeterminate` are step-local outputs never wired to the terminal verdict. The only `dropped` that reaches the verdict is what `apply_region_gated_floor` injects post-hoc (novelty-floor drops only, not the full Pass-3 drop set).
findings.location: Approach rationale / Scope ('pool every bucket the verdict carries') / What section
scenarios: The new build_payload calls `verdict.get('dropped', [])` and gets only novelty-floor drops (or an empty list), silently omitting all Pass-3 validity/veto/low-validity drops — exactly the lossless fidelity the plan claims to provide.

An integration test that synthesizes a verdict dict with a 'dropped' key will pass, but a real review that produces Pass-3 drops will not persist them because the YAML never wires them to the terminal output.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `dropped` and `indeterminate` passthrough in `code_review_coach`'s input wiring in `code-review.yaml` (passing `steps.decide.outputs.dropped` + `steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate`) and return them from the `code_review_coach` op so the terminal verdict dict carries them for `build_payload`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope: specify that `pass3_over_findings` (or `code_review_decide`) must stamp `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` onto each finding dict (alongside `decision`/`validity`/`impact`) so `build_payload` can read and persist them; these values are not currently present on the finding.
evidence: decide.py pass3_over_findings (lines 383-391): `decided.append({**f, **d, 'verification': verifs.get(i), 'tier': 'LLM'})` where `d` is `pass3_decide(...)` return dict — no block_threshold or blocking_enabled in d or the append
pass3_decide return dict (lines 305-355): keys are {decision, reason, validity, impact, priority, severity} — no threshold fields
code_review_decide op (workflow_ops.py lines 416-450) does not add block_threshold/blocking_enabled to findings either
findings.finding: The plan states each finding in the v2 payload should carry its 'resolved threshold' (`block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled`). But `pass3_over_findings` (which calls `threshold_for` per finding) does NOT stamp `block_threshold` or `blocking_enabled` onto the returned finding dict — the finding gets `{decision, reason, validity, impact, priority, severity, verification, tier}` only (decide.py line 391). So these values are not 'already carried by the finding' and cannot be read by `build_payload` without additional work.
findings.location: Why section ('does not persist the numeric `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled` each finding was judged against') / Scope ('each finding with its verification + pass-3 fields + evidence/scenarios + resolved threshold')
scenarios: build_payload calls `f.get('block_threshold')` and gets None for every finding because it was never stamped — the v2 payload omits this field silently.
Alternatively, the implementer must add threshold-stamping to pass3_over_findings or code_review_decide, but the plan gives no spec for where this should happen.
findings.suggested_fix: In `decide.py` `pass3_over_findings`, add `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` to the appended dict: `decided.append({**f, **d, 'verification': verifs.get(i), 'tier': 'LLM', 'block_threshold': block_threshold, 'blocking_enabled': blocking_enabled})`. Then `build_payload` can read them directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `overflow` from the list of buckets to pool in the v2 payload (it does not exist in the code-review gate's verdict — unlike plan-review, code-review has no advisory cap and `code_review_decide` produces no `overflow` bucket); confirm the correct bucket set is `{blocking, advisory/surfaced, coaching, dropped, indeterminate}`.
evidence: code_review_decide returns `{'decided': decided, **buckets}` where buckets = `{'blocking':[], 'surfaced':[], 'dropped':[], 'indeterminate':[]}` — no overflow key (workflow_ops.py:428-450)
src/rebar/schemas/code_review_decide_output.schema.json lists only decided/blocking/surfaced/dropped/indeterminate — no overflow property
src/rebar/schemas/code_review_verdict.schema.json has no overflow field
Plan text: 'pooling ALL buckets (surfaced + dropped + indeterminate + overflow)'
findings.finding: The plan names `overflow` as one of the buckets to pool in the v2 payload, but the code-review gate produces no `overflow` bucket — `code_review_decide` emits only `{blocking, surfaced, dropped, indeterminate}`, and the code-review verdict schema has no `overflow` field. Unlike plan-review (which applies an advisory cap creating overflow), code-review has no advisory cap, so `overflow` does not exist on a code-review verdict.
findings.location: Scope section: 'pool every bucket the verdict carries (not just blocking/advisory/coaching)' and the What section listing 'overflow'
scenarios: Implementer adds `verdict.get('overflow', [])` to build_payload but it always returns [], wasting a key slot and potentially causing confusion in downstream corpus readers.
If the plan is intended to future-proof for a cap added later, that needs to be stated explicitly — as-written it refers to an already-existing bucket that doesn't exist in code-review.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'surfaced + dropped + indeterminate + overflow' with 'surfaced (advisory) + dropped + indeterminate' throughout the plan, matching the four keys code_review_decide actually emits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `indeterminate` findings — produced by `code_review_decide` but not wired to the terminal verdict — reach `build_payload`; either thread `decide.outputs.indeterminate` through the workflow YAML to the terminal verdict (adding it as an input to `code_review_coach`), or read it from a recorder/step output rather than the verdict dict.
evidence: The `verdict` step (code_review_coach) only receives `blocking` and `surfaced` from `decide` (code-review.yaml lines 178-184); `indeterminate` is not wired.
`code_review_coach` assembles `{verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage}` — no `indeterminate` key (workflow_ops.py lines 491-509).
The plan's Scope says 'pool every bucket the verdict carries' — indeterminate never arrives on the verdict, so it silently drops to `[]`.
By contrast, `dropped` IS reachable because `apply_region_gated_floor` mutates `verdict['dropped']` after the coach step (gate_dispatch.py line 644).
findings.finding: The `indeterminate` findings produced by `code_review_decide` are never forwarded to the terminal verdict dict, so `build_payload` reading `verdict.get('indeterminate', [])` will always return an empty list — silently defeating the plan's goal of preserving the indeterminate bucket in the v2 sidecar.
scenarios: A code review where the Pass-2 verifier returns no verification for a finding (indeterminate case) produces a v2 sidecar with an empty `indeterminate` bucket even though findings were indeterminate — the calibration corpus shows false zeros.
Any reshaping violation (reshape.has_violations) degrades findings to indeterminate, but those findings vanish from the sidecar.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `indeterminate: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate }}` to the `verdict` step's `with:` block in `code-review.yaml`, pass it through `code_review_coach` to the assembled verdict dict, and then `build_payload` can read it from the verdict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm how `dropped`/`indeterminate` findings (from the `code_review_decide` step) reach the terminal verdict dict — either wire them through the `verdict` YAML step and `code_review_coach` return, or have `build_payload` read from the step recorder outputs rather than the terminal verdict alone.
evidence: plan Scope: 'in `build_payload`, pool every bucket the verdict carries (not just blocking/advisory/coaching)'
workflow_ops.py code_review_coach returns only {verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage} — no dropped/indeterminate keys
code-review.yaml verdict step: `with: blocking: ..., surfaced: ..., notes: ...` — no `dropped` or `indeterminate` wired
gate_dispatch.py line 682: `verdict = res.terminal_output` — only the terminal step's dict is the verdict
Only apply_region_gated_floor (workflow_ops.py line 644) mutates `verdict['dropped']` in-place, but that only captures novelty-region drops, not pass3 drops/indeterminate
findings.finding: The plan claims `build_payload` in v2 will pool `dropped`/`indeterminate`/`overflow` buckets from the verdict, but the terminal verdict dict produced by the `code_review_coach` workflow step only emits `{verdict, blocking, advisory, coaching, coverage}` — the `dropped` and `indeterminate` buckets from the `decide` step are not forwarded into the terminal output. Only post-floor novelty drops (added by `apply_region_gated_floor` onto the verdict in-place) would be present. Pre-floor dropped/indeterminate findings are silently absent from the verdict dict that `build_payload` receives.
findings.location: Scope section ('in `build_payload`, pool every bucket the verdict carries') and src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:491-509 (`code_review_coach` step return dict) and src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml (verdict step does not wire `indeterminate`/`dropped` inputs)
scenarios: A reviewer implements `build_payload` to call `verdict.get('dropped', [])` and `verdict.get('indeterminate', [])` — these will always be empty (except novelty-floor drops) because the terminal verdict dict never carries pass-3 dropped/indeterminate findings.

The integration test in AC1 passes a hand-crafted verdict dict with all buckets pre-populated, so it passes, but production emits from `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` will silently drop those buckets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `dropped`/`indeterminate` as passthrough inputs to the `verdict` step in `code-review.yaml` and update `code_review_coach` in `workflow_ops.py` to include them in its return dict alongside `blocking`/`advisory`/`coaching`/`coverage`. Then `build_payload` can read them from the verdict as planned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled` per-finding persistence is also intended for the plan-review sidecar (and thus needs a coordinated bump there too), or acknowledge it as a deliberate code-review-specific addition and update the Approach rationale to remove the 'one reader shape' claim.
evidence: plan Why: 'It also does not persist the numeric `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled` each finding was judged against'
plan Approach rationale: 'so both share one fidelity standard and one reader shape'

plan_review/sidecar.py _slim() persists: id, norm_id, location, criteria, cohort, tier, decision, severity, validity, impact, priority, reason, drop_reason, verification, finding, suggested_fix, checklist_item — no block_threshold or blocking_enabled
ABSENCE: `block_threshold` does not appear anywhere in plan_review/sidecar.py
findings.finding: The plan asserts that persisting 'resolved `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled`' per finding matches plan-review sidecar fidelity, but `plan_review/sidecar.py`'s `_slim()` function does NOT persist `block_threshold` or `blocking_enabled`. These fields are absent from `plan_review_result_v1`. The plan's parity claim is unverified — the code-review v2 would actually introduce a field the plan-review sidecar doesn't have, making the two sidecars diverge in shape rather than converge.
findings.location: Why/What sections ('the numeric `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled` each finding was judged against') and src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py `_slim()` function (lines 308-351)
scenarios: An implementer adds `block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled` to the code-review v2 `_slim()` equivalent but not to plan-review's — the two sidecars then have different shapes, contradicting the 'one reader shape' rationale.
Alternatively, the implementer omits the field to match plan-review, silently dropping a key stated AC (AC1: 'each finding with its verification, pass-3 determination, and resolved threshold').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's bucket enumeration for code-review v2 to remove `overflow` (a plan-review-only bucket absent from the code-review verdict shape) or explicitly specify adding `overflow` to the code-review gate if it is intentional.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'pooling ALL buckets (surfaced + dropped + indeterminate + overflow)'
AC1: 'payload includes those buckets' listing 'dropped/indeterminate'
code_review_decide returns {decided, blocking, surfaced, dropped, indeterminate} — no overflow
plan_review/sidecar.py line 356: verdict.get('overflow', []) — a plan-review-only bucket
code_review_verdict.schema.json has no overflow property
findings.finding: The plan references an `overflow` bucket for the code-review gate that does not exist in the code-review verdict shape. The 'What' section enumerates 'dropped / indeterminate / overflow findings' to pool, and AC1 repeats this. But `code_review_decide` (workflow_ops.py lines 428-450) emits only `blocking`, `surfaced`, `dropped`, and `indeterminate` — there is no `overflow` bucket. The code_review_verdict.schema.json also has no `overflow` property. This is a plan-review-only construct (plan_review/sidecar.py line 356 reads `verdict.get('overflow', [])`).
findings.location: Plan 'What' section; AC1 ('dropped / indeterminate / overflow'); src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py lines 428-450; src/rebar/schemas/code_review_verdict.schema.json
scenarios: An executor implementing 'pool every bucket the verdict carries' will read verdict.get('overflow', []) and get an empty list, producing either dead code or a false sense of completeness. Any downstream calibration consumer expecting an overflow key will find it absent or always empty.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section and AC1, replace 'dropped / indeterminate / overflow' with 'dropped / indeterminate' to match the actual code_review_decide output. If overflow is genuinely desired, add it to code_review_decide and the verdict schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section the field names (e.g. `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`) and WHERE the resolved threshold is stamped — either pre-stamped on each finding dict by `code_review_decide` before returning, or re-resolved in `build_payload` via a `threshold_for` call — so the executor has an unambiguous implementation path.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'each finding's full pass-2 verification and pass-3 determination, plus the resolved threshold/blocking_enabled'
plan_review/sidecar.py _slim() does not include block_threshold or blocking_enabled fields

registry.threshold_for() returns a (block_threshold, blocking_enabled) tuple but is called inside pass3_over_findings, not available at sidecar emit time unless re-called or pre-stamped on the finding
The code-review verdict findings dict has no block_threshold/blocking_enabled field currently
findings.finding: The plan says each finding in the v2 payload should carry its 'resolved threshold' (`block_threshold`/`blocking_enabled`), but never specifies: (a) the field names to use in the finding dict, (b) how/where the resolver is called to stamp these (the per-finding `threshold_for(criteria)` call is in `pass3_over_findings`, not in `build_payload`), or (c) whether the threshold is stamped at emit time or pre-stamped on the verdict findings. The plan-review `_slim()` reference implementation (plan_review/sidecar.py lines 308-351) does NOT persist `block_threshold` or `blocking_enabled` — so the executor has no existing template.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section ('plus the resolved threshold/blocking_enabled'); AC1 ('each finding with its … resolved threshold'); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py lines 308-351
scenarios: An executor reading the plan will be uncertain whether to: call threshold_for() again in build_payload for each finding (a second registry read), add block_threshold/blocking_enabled to each finding dict in code_review_decide before returning, or add a new top-level field to the payload. Different choices produce incompatible v2 schemas.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_latest_payload_with_ts` must be updated to accept BOTH `code_review_result_v1` and `code_review_result_v2` (e.g. `payload.get('schema') in {'code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2'}`), not only the new `SCHEMA` constant, to honour the stated backward-compat guarantee.
evidence: Current guard: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` — a strict equality check (sidecar.py line 218).
After bumping `SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v2'`, every v1 sidecar payload fails this check and is silently skipped.
The plan's Compatibility section states 'v1 code-review sidecars remain readable' but does not specify updating the guard.

The Scope section names `latest_code_review_result` as the place to add dual-version acceptance, but the underlying `_latest_payload_with_ts` reader (which does the actual schema check) is not mentioned.
findings.finding: Bumping the `SCHEMA` constant to `code_review_result_v2` causes `_latest_payload_with_ts` (line 218 of sidecar.py) to silently reject all existing v1 sidecars via its hardcoded equality guard `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`, breaking the plan's stated backward-compat guarantee and AC2's 'reads both a v1 and a v2' contract.
findings.location: Scope section ('latest_code_review_result accepts both code_review_result_v1 and _v2') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py line 218 / AC2
scenarios: After deployment, the first v2 sidecar emit succeeds, but all prior v1 sidecars become invisible to `latest_code_review_result` because `_latest_payload_with_ts` rejects them — the region-gated floor loses its prior-findings memory and runs un-narrowed.
The AC2 unit test passes (it uses hand-written records) but the production reader silently drops v1 records after schema bump.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `KNOWN_SCHEMAS = frozenset({'code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2'})` and replace the equality guard in `_latest_payload_with_ts` with `payload.get('schema') in KNOWN_SCHEMAS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add evidence/scenarios to the AC1 oracle assertion so the integration test explicitly verifies those fields are present in the emitted v2 event JSON.
evidence: Scope: 'each finding with its verification + pass-3 fields + evidence/scenarios + resolved threshold'
AC1 oracle: 'asserting on the emitted event JSON' for 'verification, pass-3 determination, and resolved threshold' — evidence/scenarios not listed
findings.finding: The Scope section names 'evidence/scenarios' as a per-finding field to be persisted in v2, but no Acceptance Criterion oracle explicitly asserts that evidence/scenarios are present in the emitted payload — AC1 only checks 'verification, pass-3 determination, and resolved threshold'.
scenarios: An implementer writes a test that passes without including evidence/scenarios in the payload, satisfying AC1 literally but not delivering the described scope item
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1 oracle sentence to: '…asserting on the emitted event JSON for verification, pass-3 determination, resolved threshold, evidence, and scenarios fields per finding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section that `_latest_payload_with_ts` (or an equivalent inner reader) must accept BOTH schema strings (`code_review_result_v1` and `code_review_result_v2`), not just the new constant, to satisfy the v1-readable compatibility guarantee.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'latest_code_review_result accepts both code_review_result_v1 and _v2'
sidecar.py line 218: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` — strict equality on the module constant
sidecar.py line 22: `SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v1'` — to be bumped to v2 per plan
If SCHEMA becomes 'code_review_result_v2', any v1 sidecar returns (None, -1) from _latest_payload_with_ts
findings.finding: The plan requires `latest_code_review_result` to accept both v1 and v2 sidecars (AC2), but the internal `_latest_payload_with_ts` helper — which is the actual schema-guarded reader `latest_code_review_result` delegates to — guards on `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`. Bumping `SCHEMA` to `code_review_result_v2` would make this guard reject all v1 records, breaking backward compatibility. The plan specifies the desired outcome but not the implementation mechanism for the dual-version guard.
findings.location: Scope section ('latest_code_review_result accepts both code_review_result_v1 and _v2'); AC2; src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py lines 194-225
scenarios: After the bump, a repo with only v1 sidecars (existing deployments before backfill) will have latest_code_review_result return None, causing the novelty floor to run un-narrowed on every review until a v2 sidecar is written — defeating the 'v1 sidecars remain readable' guarantee in the Compatibility section.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet: '_latest_payload_with_ts should guard on `schema in {"code_review_result_v1", "code_review_result_v2"}` rather than a single constant, so existing v1 sidecars remain readable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1's oracle to enumerate the minimum assertion surface: for at least one dropped and one indeterminate finding in the fixture, assert via `jq -e` (or equivalent) that `verification`, `pass3_determination`, and `block_threshold` fields are present and non-null on each finding within those buckets.
evidence: Oracle description: 'asserting on the emitted event JSON'

AC1 body names the required fields ('each finding with its verification, pass-3 determination, and resolved threshold') but the oracle sentence does not repeat them, leaving the assertion surface implicit
Defect taxonomy #4: fixture invalidity — 'the fixture does not actually exercise the claim'
Defect taxonomy #5: missing cardinality / completeness assertion when the criterion names specific sub-fields
findings.finding: AC1's oracle ('asserting on the emitted event JSON') does not enumerate which specific keys must be present in the assertion, creating a fixture-invalidity risk: a test that only checks bucket-name presence but omits assertions on pass-3 fields, verification, and resolved threshold would satisfy the prose while bypassing the criterion's actual intent.
scenarios: An executor writes a test that checks `dropped` key exists in the payload but does not assert on `verification`, `pass3_determination`, or `block_threshold` fields per finding — CI goes green, criterion is not proven
A future reviewer cannot tell from the AC alone what the integration test must structurally assert
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 oracle sentence: 'The test must assert (e.g. via `jq -e`) that at least one finding in the `dropped` bucket and one in the `indeterminate` bucket each carry non-null `verification`, `pass3_determination`, and `block_threshold` values in the emitted event JSON.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback plan to address v2 records written before revert: either split the v2-reader deploy from the v2-writer deploy (expand-contract), or explicitly state and justify that the bounded unreadability window is acceptable.
evidence: 'reverting restores the v1 payload' — this only describes the writer side; reader side post-rollback is unaddressed
'latest_code_review_result accepts both code_review_result_v1 and _v2' lives in the same changeset as the writer, so revert removes both simultaneously
findings.finding: The rollback path 'reverting restores the v1 payload' does not address the fate of v2 records already written during the deployment window: after a revert, the reverted reader (which no longer understands v2) cannot read those records. The plan needs to either (a) stage the v2-reader as a separate, earlier deploy that survives rollback, or (b) explicitly acknowledge the bounded unreadability window and accept it as a justified trade-off.
scenarios: v2 records are emitted, a defect is discovered, code is reverted — latest_code_review_result (reverted to v1-only) now silently fails or errors on the v2 records in the store, breaking the novelty-floor gate until those records age out
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Any v2 records written before rollback will be unreadable by the reverted reader until they age out of the look-back window; this window is bounded by [TTL/look-back duration] and is accepted as the rollback cost. Alternatively, deploy the v2-reader as a no-op prerequisite commit so it survives a writer-only revert.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py` as a required update: its hardcoded `SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v1'` guard must be widened (e.g., accept both v1 and v2, or updated to v2) so the corpus reader continues to load sidecar events after the schema bump.
evidence: docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py:36: `SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v1'`
docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py:102: `if not isinstance(payload, dict) or payload.get('schema') != SCHEMA: continue`
This file is outside src/rebar/llm/code_review/ and is not mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: `docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py` is an unenumerated consumer of the `SCHEMA` constant: it hardcodes `SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v1'` and guards its corpus loader with `payload.get('schema') != SCHEMA`, so any v2 sidecar written after the bump will be silently skipped by this calibration script.
findings.location: Scope section (lists only `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` as the edit target); docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py:36,102
scenarios: After the v2 bump, all new code-review sidecar events write schema='code_review_result_v2'; the threshold-derivation script's corpus reader silently drops every v2 sidecar, yielding an empty corpus and a misleading 'no threshold found' result when re-run.
findings.suggested_fix: In derive_code_review_threshold.py, change the guard from `payload.get('schema') != SCHEMA` to `payload.get('schema') not in {'code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2'}`, or add a comment that it must be updated in lockstep with any schema version bump.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws4.py` to the edit scope and update the hardcoded `'code_review_result_v1'` schema assertion at line 210 to `'code_review_result_v2'` (or reference the `SCHEMA` constant directly).
evidence: tests/unit/test_code_review_ws4.py:210: `assert payload['schema'] == 'code_review_result_v1'`
Plan AC4: `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass — this test would break the suite
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws4.py:210` asserts `payload['schema'] == 'code_review_result_v1'` by hardcoded string; when `SCHEMA` is bumped to `code_review_result_v2`, this test will fail. The plan does not enumerate this test as a required update.
findings.location: Scope section (only names `sidecar.py` as the edit target); tests/unit/test_code_review_ws4.py:210
scenarios: After bumping SCHEMA in sidecar.py, test_sidecar_payload_anchors_on_the_explicit_target_ticket fails on the schema assertion, breaking `make test`.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `assert payload['schema'] == 'code_review_result_v1'` to `assert payload['schema'] == sidecar.SCHEMA` so the assertion tracks the constant automatically on future bumps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-rollback schema state for already-written v2 sidecar events: either (a) document that the rolled-back `latest_code_review_result` must retain v2-reading capability (keep the reader change non-reverted), or (b) specify a compensating-forward path (e.g., treat an unrecognised schema version as 'no prior result' and re-run), explicitly noting the DATA LOSS RISK of (b).
evidence: Plan states: 'reverting restores the v1 payload' — describes future-write behaviour only, silent on already-persisted v2 records
Plan states: 'latest_code_review_result accepts both code_review_result_v1 and _v2' — this acceptance is LOST on rollback
No mention of what `latest_code_review_result` returns or does when it encounters a v2 record after rollback (the record may be the most-recent for a given code review)
POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: the store may contain v2 records while the rolled-back reader only handles v1
findings.finding: The rollback description only asserts that future emits revert to the v1 payload shape, but does not address the schema state of already-written v2 sidecar events. After a rollback, the reverted reader (`latest_code_review_result`) loses its v2-reading capability, leaving any v2 records written during the forward-deployment window unreadable — the plan does not assert on or mitigate this post-rollback intermediate state.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section — 'reverting restores the v1 payload'
scenarios: A code review is run after forward deploy, writing a v2 sidecar. A rollback is then performed. The novelty-floor reader or gate now calls `latest_code_review_result`, which (reverted) only understands v1; it either errors, returns None, or silently skips the v2 record — causing the gate to behave as if no prior review exists for that change.
Automated pipelines that continuously re-evaluate code reviews could fail or produce incorrect results for all reviews conducted in the deployment window.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compatibility & rollback section, add: 'After rollback, any v2 sidecars written during the deployment window remain in the store. The rolled-back reader will encounter unknown schema version `code_review_result_v2`; specify that it must treat an unknown version as `None` (triggering a fresh review) rather than erroring, and document this as a known post-rollback behaviour.' Alternatively, hold the reader change as a non-reverted shim so v2 records remain readable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design-doc or CLAUDE.md entry (or inline ADR) capturing the shared-fidelity-contract decision so future sidecar authors have a canonical reference.
evidence: 'Mirror story A's plan-review enrichment on the code-review sidecar so both share one fidelity standard and one reader shape'
No mention of ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The shared-schema contract is a cross-component architectural decision affecting both sidecar families and any future reader implementors
findings.finding: The plan introduces a shared cross-sidecar fidelity contract ('one fidelity standard and one reader shape' for both plan-review and code-review sidecars) and a new schema version, but does not mention updating any architectural documentation (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc) to record this decision.
scenarios: A future engineer adding a third sidecar type (e.g., test-review) has no documented rationale for why the shared fidelity shape was chosen, leading to inconsistent design choices
Readers of code_review_result_v2 have no canonical reference for what 'all buckets + resolved threshold' means contractually
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single paragraph to CLAUDE.md (or a lightweight ADR in docs/adr/) stating: 'Both plan-review and code-review sidecars use the same all-buckets + resolved-threshold fidelity model. When adding a new review-type sidecar, follow code_review_result_v2 as the canonical shape.' This can be a 3–5 sentence entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a runtime flag (e.g. `CODE_REVIEW_SIDECAR_SCHEMA_VERSION` env var or feature flag) that gates v2 emission so the rollout can be staged and v1 can be restored without a full redeploy.
evidence: 'reverting restores the v1 payload' — rollback is a full code revert, not a flag-disable
No mention of a feature flag, canary percentage, or staged rollout gating the v2 emission
Plan says 'No backfill' and 'v1 sidecars remain readable', confirming no deploy-ordering issue for reads, but the write-side flip is 100% at deploy time
findings.finding: The plan bumps the sidecar schema from v1 to v2 for all code-review runs at once with no feature flag, canary, or staged rollout. The only rollback path is a code revert, which is cheap and requires no data cleanup, but the criterion requires a staged rollout mechanism so a partial rollout / quick-disable path exists independently of a full revert.
scenarios: A bug in build_payload for dropped/indeterminate buckets corrupts all emitted sidecars immediately at rollout with no partial-traffic escape hatch
An on-call engineer needs to disable v2 emission without a full redeploy during an incident
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a config/env var `CODE_REVIEW_SIDECAR_V2=true/false` checked in `build_payload`; default it to `false` in the first deploy, validate v2 output via the integration test in a canary environment, then flip to `true`. This gives an instant rollback knob without a code change.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
